none: Woman sits at desk, looks at man

body.creator: Ada
body.value: Reynolds flares up at Cyril’s use of the word “chic.” For him, his art stands above all other concerns, especially financial ones. Taste defines his aesthetics and his life, so the news that Henrietta Harding has gone to another fashion house offends him.  While Reynolds would rather have nothing to do with the financial side of his enterprise, which his sister maintains, and remain above it all in the realm of a pure aesthetics divorced from considerations of money, he nonetheless clearly benefits from the haute couture world in which he operates. Cyril's words underscore that Reynolds the artist cannot divorce himself from that equation. In this way, Reynolds is not unlike other artist figures in PTA’s filmography such as Jack Horner of Boogie Nights whose desire to maintain artistic integrity and vision bump up against commercial pressures and shifts in the marketplace. It’s easy to see a little bit of PTA with his idiosyncratic filmmaking in these figures. This moment in Phantom Thread also connects to the real-life figure of Cristóbal Balenciaga, who, his biographer writes, “was obsessively perfecting his line rather than changing it with each season” (Blume, The Master of Us All, 146).Catherine Join-Diéterle also writes that the Spanish designer “expected the greatest loyalty of his clients, since this was not an image that could change with a woman’s mood. How was he to understand that there were those who might dress themselves from more than one fashion house? The attention he lavished upon his clients was suddenly undermined when, attracted by [Dior’s] New Look, several of them abandoned him for a time. Hurt, he stopped going to see them during fittings” (Join-Diéterle, “Dior and Balenciaga,” 14
